correctness.justification: All 17/17 acceptance tests pass. Implementation correctly handles recursive merging, override precedence, list replacement (not concatenation), None as regular value, mixed-type override, and all empty-dict combinations. The two-loop approach (base keys then override-only keys) is correct.

edge_cases.justification: All spec edge cases handled: both empty, one empty, None override, dict-to-scalar, scalar-to-dict, 3+ level nesting, list replacement. No-mutation guarantee verified via copy.deepcopy on all leaf values. Result independence confirmed by tests that mutate result and verify originals unchanged.

code_quality.justification: Clean and readable at 38 lines. Type hints on function signature. Good use of copy.deepcopy for independence guarantee. The result type hint uses dict[str, Any] but the function accepts dict (any key type) -- minor inconsistency. Could use a single iteration with set union of keys for slightly cleaner logic, but the two-loop approach is perfectly clear.

documentation.justification: Good module docstring and function docstring with Args and Returns sections. Clearly explains the override precedence and no-mutation guarantee. Missing: Raises section (function doesn't raise but documenting that both inputs must be dicts would be helpful), usage examples in the docstring.

test_coverage.justification: 16 well-structured tests covering: basic no-overlap, scalar override, recursive merge, 3-level deep, list replacement, None handling, both mixed-type directions, all empty combinations, no-mutation of base, no-mutation of override, result independence, nested independence, and None-doesn't-delete. Thorough coverage of the spec.

robustness.justification: Correctly uses copy.deepcopy to prevent mutation and ensure independence. However, no input validation -- passing non-dict arguments (e.g. deep_merge(None, {})) would raise an unhelpful AttributeError rather than a clear ValueError. The spec says 'both inputs must be dicts' in scope boundary but the implementation doesn't validate this. No protection against very deeply nested inputs (recursion limit).
